title: The Reasons We Don’t Learn a Language
summaryMarkdown:
## Core claim
The essay argues that language learning usually fails not because people lack the right method, but because they avoid the sustained practice required for meaningful progress. Since language learning takes years, has moving goalposts, and rarely offers a clean finish line, people protect themselves with stories that make stopping feel reasonable.

## The stories learners tell
The writer examines a series of familiar excuses: that language learning is too hard, that fluency should have arrived already, that age or lack of time makes effort pointless, that fatigue makes practice impossible, that learning is too expensive, that AI makes it unnecessary, or that some people simply are not 'language people.' Each excuse is treated less as a factual barrier than as a way of avoiding vulnerability, frustration, and comparison.

## Time, culture, and real communication
A major theme is scale: learners underestimate how much exposure real fluency requires, especially when compared with the thousands of hours of input children receive. The essay also argues that language is not just vocabulary and grammar but a cultural way of interacting, apologizing, complimenting, respecting, and understanding one another, which is why translation tools and literal equivalence miss something essential.

## Final message
In the final section, the barriers to learning are named as largely emotional: fear of looking foolish, fear of wasting time, and fear of not being good enough. That vulnerability is precisely what makes language learning worthwhile, because it forces people to become beginners again and keep showing up without magical shortcuts.
